html:
[1620]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以射猎为业 - Means 'to make hunting one's livelihood,' indicating a profession centered around hunting.
  • 与人逐鹿入山 - Means 'to chase deer with others into the mountains,' describing an activity that involves both competition and exploration.
  • 忽有白气去地十丈许 - Describes a sudden appearance of white vapor at a height of about ten zhang (a traditional Chinese unit of length, approximately 3.3 meters), which is an unusual and supernatural occurrence.
  • 若有所中洒血布地 - Means 'if it hit something, blood would spread on the ground,' suggesting a hit that results in visible bloodshed.
  • 闻空中语云正中大王 - Means 'he heard a voice from the air saying it hit the Great King,' indicating a supernatural voice that confirms the hit.
  • 俄见一大蛇挂树身有箭 - Means 'soon he saw a large snake hanging from a tree with an arrow in its body,' describing a dramatic and unexpected sight.
  • 顷刻有群蛇辏辐向莽 - Means 'in a moment, a group of snakes gathered around Teng,' indicating a rapid and coordinated movement of snakes.
  • 盘绕莽身咂咂有声 - Means 'they wrapped around Teng's body, making a sucking sound,' describing the snakes' behavior as they encircle him.
  • 须臾散去 - Means 'in a short while, they dispersed,' indicating a sudden departure of the snakes.
  • 唯见一聚白骨 - Means 'he only saw a pile of white bones,' describing the final state after the snakes leave.
  • 同生三子年将弱冠皆有声无言 - Means 'they had three sons who were nearly of age but could make sounds but not speak,' describing the sons' condition.
  • 君可内省何以致此 - Means 'you should reflect internally on what caused this,' suggesting introspection.
  • 昔为小儿时当牀上有燕巢中有三子 - Means 'when we were children, there was a swallow's nest on the bed with three chicks,' providing a past event.
  • 其母从外得哺三子皆出口受之 - Means 'the mother brought food from outside, and all three chicks opened their mouths to receive it,' describing a nurturing scene.
  • 试以指内巢中燕雏亦出口受之 - Means 'try putting your finger into the nest, and the swallow chicks will also open their mouths to receive it,' suggesting a test of trust.
  • 因以三蔷茨食之既而皆死 - Means 'then feed them three thorns, and they will all die,' describing a fatal outcome.
  • 言讫其三子之言语忽然周稳 - Means 'after he finished speaking, the three sons suddenly began to speak clearly and steadily,' indicating a miraculous change.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 陈莽 - The name of the main character, a hunter from Linchuan.
  • 临川 - A place name, referring to Linchuan, a region in Jiangxi Province.
  • 沛国 - A place name, referring to Pei, a region in Henan Province.
  • 弱冠 - Refers to the age of 20, the traditional age of manhood in ancient China.
  • 燕巢 - Refers to a swallow's nest, a common feature in traditional Chinese homes.
  • 蔷茨 - Refers to thorns, a plant with sharp points that can cause harm.

Proper Nouns

  • 陈莽 - The name of the hunter from Linchuan.
  • 临川 - The place name Linchuan.
  • 沛国 - The place name Pei.
  • 燕巢 - The place name of the swallow's nest.
  • 蔷茨 - The plant name thorns.

[1621]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 奉朝請 - A title or honorific for retired officials who are invited to attend court ceremonies but not required to perform official duties.
  • 非手殺牛則噉之不美 - A conditional phrase meaning 'either he kills the cow himself or he does not eat it, it is not good.'
  • 病笃 - Means 'severe illness' or 'on the brink of death.'
  • 舉體如被刀刺 - A vivid description meaning 'his whole body felt as if it were being stabbed by knives.'
  • 見大牛來 - Means 'he saw a big cow coming.'
  • 向幽州淀中捕鱼 - Means 'fished in the ponds near Youzhou.'
  • 囓之而死 - Means 'bit him and he died.'
  • 登树摆杀之 - Means 'climbed the tree and shook it to kill the monkey.'
  • 化为大虫 - Means 'transformed into a big beast' (where '大虫' can also mean 'tiger').
  • 入山不知所在 - Means 'went into the mountains and was never seen again.'
  • 与妇佃于野舍 - Means 'farmed with his wife in a rural cottage.'
  • 五色光鲜 - Means 'brightly colored.'
  • 饷之 - Means 'fed it to him.'
  • 密打杀 - Means 'secretly killed.'
  • 能食之病 - Means 'a disease that makes one eat excessively.'
  • 三斛饭犹不为饱 - Means 'three measures of rice were still not enough to satisfy him.'
  • 少时而死 - Means 'died shortly after.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 齐国 - Refers to the ancient state of Qi.
  • 江陵 - Refers to the city of Jiangling, an important location in Chinese history.
  • 高伟 - A person's name, likely a historical or fictional figure.
  • 社树 - Refers to a sacred tree in a shrine or temple.
  • 幽州淀中 - Refers to a region in northern China, specifically the area around modern-day Beijing.
  • 述异记 - A classical text that records strange and unusual events.
  • 异苑 - Another classical text that documents peculiar occurrences and supernatural events.

Proper Nouns

  • 齐朝请 - A title or honorific.
  • 高伟 - A person's name.
  • 伍寺之 - A person's name.
  • 苏巷 - A person's name.
  • 述异记 - A classical text.
  • 异苑 - A classical text.

[1622]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 性特忍害 - Describes a person with a particularly cruel or harmful nature.
  • 探牛舌本割之以归为炙食之 - A complex phrase describing the act of cutting off a cow's tongue while drunk and taking it home to roast and eat it.
  • 后乃深起悔责求道度禅师发大誓愿罄舍家资回买此地为立伽蓝 - A complex phrase describing the person's deep remorse, seeking the path, and making a vow to the Zen master to give up all his wealth to buy back the land and build a temple.
  • 乃炙鱼眼以厌之信宿之间珠便出 - Means 'he then roasted fish eyes to ward off the evil, and after a few days, the pearl reappeared.'
  • 及醒即觉体痒爬搔隐疹因尔成癞十许年死 - Means 'when he woke up, he felt itchy and scratched, developing rashes that eventually turned into leprosy, and he died after about ten years.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 阮倪 - Likely a historical or legendary figure, possibly from a local legend or historical record.
  • 邵文立 - Likely a historical or legendary figure, possibly from a local legend or historical record.
  • 梁元帝 - Historical figure, Emperor Yuan of Liang (508–554), a ruler of the Southern Dynasties.
  • 梁孝元 - Historical figure, Emperor Yuan of Liang (508–554), also known as Xiao Yi.
  • 刘敬躬 - Historical figure, a rebel leader during the Southern Dynasties period.
  • 望蔡县令 - Historical figure, a local official during the Southern Dynasties period.
  • 伽蓝 - A term for a Buddhist temple or monastery.

Proper Nouns

  • 阮倪 - A person's name.
  • 邵文立 - A person's name.
  • 梁元帝 - Historical figure, Emperor Yuan of Liang (508–554).
  • 梁孝元 - Historical figure, Emperor Yuan of Liang (508–554).
  • 刘敬躬 - Historical figure, a rebel leader.
  • 望蔡县令 - Historical figure, a local official.
  • 伽蓝 - A term for a Buddhist temple or monastery.

[1623]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 后周武帝时 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, a specific historical period.
  • 敷州义阳寺 - A specific location, the Yiyang Temple in Fuzhou.
  • 恒遣沙弥及奴放于山谷 - "恒" means "always" or "constantly"; "遣" means "send"; "沙弥" refers to a novice monk; "奴" refers to a slave or servant; "放" means "to let loose" or "to release".
  • 频有人来驱逐此羊 - "频" means "frequently" or "often"; "驱逐" means "to drive away".
  • 汝之畜养猪羊其罪最甚 - "畜养" means "to raise" or "to keep"; "猪羊" refers to pigs and sheep; "其罪最甚" means "the greatest sin".
  • 何劳防惜 - "何劳" means "why bother"; "防惜" means "to guard and cherish".
  • 灭法教资财并送官府 - "灭法" means "abolition of Buddhism"; "教资财" means "religious property"; "并送官府" means "and handed over to the government".
  • 公私牵挽并皆分散 - "公私" means "public and private"; "牵挽" means "to pull" or "to drag"; "并皆分散" means "all were dispersed".
  • 释僧羣 - Refers to a monk named Seng Qun.
  • 清贫守节 - Means "living a simple and virtuous life".
  • 蔬食持经 - "蔬食" means "vegetarian diet"; "持经" means "to hold or recite scriptures".
  • 居罗江县之霍山 - "罗江县" refers to Luojian County; "霍山" refers to Huo Mountain.
  • 搆立茅室 - "搆立" means "to build"; "茅室" means "a hut made of straw".
  • 孤在海中 - "孤" means "isolated"; "在海中" means "in the sea".
  • 上有石盂水深六尺 - "石盂" means "stone basin"; "水深六尺" means "water six feet deep".
  • 古老相传是羣仙所宅 - "古老相传" means "passed down by ancient people"; "羣仙所宅" means "the abode of a group of immortals".
  • 绝粒 - Means "to abstain from food".
  • 庵舍与石防隔一小涧 - "庵舍" means "hermitage"; "石防" means "stone fence"; "一小涧" means "a small stream".
  • 常以木为梁 - "常" means "always"; "以木为梁" means "to use wood as a beam".
  • 由之以汲水 - "由之" means "through it"; "汲水" means "to draw water".
  • 折翅鸭 - "折翅" means "broken wing"; "鸭" means "duck".
  • 舒翼当梁头 - "舒翼" means "to spread wings"; "当梁头" means "in front of the beam".
  • 将举锡拨之 - "举锡" means "to lift the staff"; "拨之" means "to push it away".
  • 恐有转伤 - "恐" means "fear"; "有转伤" means "to cause injury".
  • 因此回归 - "因此" means "therefore"; "回归" means "to return".
  • 遂绝水数日而终 - "遂" means "then"; "绝水" means "to abstain from water"; "数日" means "several days"; "而终" means "and died".
  • 我少时曾折一鸭翅 - "我少时" means "when I was young"; "曾折一鸭翅" means "once broke a duck's wing".
  • 验此以为报也 - "验此" means "to verify this"; "以为报也" means "as a form of retribution".

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 后周武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, who reigned from 561 to 578 AD.
  • 敷州义阳寺 - A specific temple located in Fuzhou, a city in present-day Shanxi Province.
  • 灭法 - Refers to the persecution of Buddhism during the reign of Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou.
  • 释僧羣 - Refers to a monk named Seng Qun, who lived during the late Northern and Southern Dynasties period.
  • 罗江县之霍山 - Refers to Huo Mountain in Luojian County, a specific location in Sichuan Province.
  • 羣仙 - Refers to a group of immortals, a common theme in Chinese mythology and folklore.

[1624]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 冀州外邑 - Refers to a small town outside the administrative center of Jizhou (冀州).
  • 翌日侵旦 - Means 'the next morning early.'
  • 叩门呼 - Means 'knocking on the door and calling out.'
  • 官唤汝 - Means 'the official is calling you.'
  • 入取衣粮 - Means 'to enter and take your clothes and provisions.'
  • 不须也 - Means 'not necessary.'
  • 引儿去 - Means 'to lead the child away.'
  • 旧是桑田 - Means 'was formerly a mulberry field.'
  • 耕讫未下种 - Means 'plowed but not yet sown.'
  • 烧煨食之 - Means 'roasted and ate them.'
  • 城门忽闭 - Means 'the city gate suddenly closed.'
  • 不见一人 - Means 'no one was seen.'
  • 唯是空城 - Means 'only an empty city.'
  • 地皆灰碎火 - Means 'the ground was covered with ashes and broken fire.'
  • 深才没踝 - Means 'deep enough to cover the ankles.'
  • 垂至即闭 - Means 'as soon as he reached it, it closed.'
  • 走趋东西 - Means 'ran towards the east and west.'
  • 未到则开 - Means 'if he hadn't arrived, it would open.'
  • 既至便阖 - Means 'once he arrived, it closed.'
  • 村人出田采桑 - Means 'villagers went out to the fields to pick mulberries.'
  • 男女甚众 - Means 'many men and women.'
  • 皆见儿在耕田中啼泣 - Means 'all saw the child crying in the field.'
  • 四方驰走 - Means 'rushed from all directions.'
  • 相谓曰 - Means 'said to each other.'
  • 此儿狂耶 - Means 'Is this child crazy?'
  • 旦来如此游戏不息 - Means 'in the morning he played like this without stopping.'
  • 至于食时 - Means 'by the time of meals.'
  • 采者皆归 - Means 'the pickers all returned.'
  • 儿父问曰 - Means 'the child's father asked.'
  • 见吾儿否 - Means 'did you see my son?'
  • 桑人答曰 - Means 'the mulberry picker replied.'
  • 父儿在村南走戏唤不肯来 - Means 'Father, the child is running and playing in the south of the village and won't come when called.'
  • 父出村外遥见儿走 - Means 'the father went out of the village and saw the child running from afar.'
  • 大呼其名一声便住 - Means 'he shouted the child's name, and the child stopped.'
  • 城灰忽然不见 - Means 'the city's ashes suddenly disappeared.'
  • 父儿倒号泣言之 - Means 'the father and child fell and wept.'
  • 视其足半胫已上血肉燋干 - Means 'they looked at his feet, which were half-shin and above, and the flesh was scorched and dried.'
  • 膝已下红烂如炙 - Means 'below the knee, it was red and rotten like roasted meat.'
  • 抱归养疗 - Means 'they carried him back to be treated.'
  • 髀已上肉如故 - Means 'the thigh and above were as usual.'
  • 膝已下遂为枯骨 - Means 'below the knee, it became dry bone.'
  • 邻里闻之看其走处足迹通利 - Means 'neighbors heard about it and saw the path where he had run, which was smooth.'
  • 了无灰火 - Means 'there were no ashes or fire.'
  • 良因实业触处见狱 - Means 'likely due to his real nature, he saw prisons everywhere.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 隋开皇初 - Refers to the early years of the Sui dynasty (581-618 CE) during the reign of Emperor Wen (开皇).
  • 冀州 - An ancient Chinese province, one of the Nine Provinces of China.
  • 道惠法师 - A Buddhist monk who lived during the Sui dynasty and is known for his travels and teachings.
  • 防报记 - A collection of stories or records, possibly a Buddhist text or a local chronicle.

Proper Nouns

  • 隋开皇初 - The early years of the Sui dynasty.
  • 冀州 - An ancient Chinese province.
  • 道惠法师 - A Buddhist monk from Jizhou.
  • 防报记 - A collection of stories or records.

[1625]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 太平广记 - A collection of tales and anecdotes compiled during the Song Dynasty, covering a wide range of topics including historical events, biographies, and supernatural stories.
  • 卷一百三十一 - The specific volume and chapter number of the *Tai Ping Guang Ji*.
  • 骁骑将军 - A military rank in ancient China, indicating a high-ranking general.
  • 代郡 - An ancient Chinese region, now part of Shanxi Province.
  • 隋开皇末年 - The end of the Kaihuang era during the Sui Dynasty (581-618 AD).
  • 蒲州 - An ancient Chinese city, now part of Shanxi Province.
  • 畋猎 - Hunting, often with a connotation of recreational or leisurely pursuit.
  • 五男无女 - Having five sons but no daughters.
  • 端美 - Beautiful and virtuous.
  • 见者皆爱怜之 - Everyone who sees her loves and pities her.
  • 父母尤钟爱 - Parents especially cherish and love her.
  • 还乡里 - Return to the hometown.
  • 七岁 - Seven years old.
  • 失所在 - Lost or disappeared.
  • 邻里戏藏匿之 - Neighbors playfully hide her.
  • 访问不见 - Search but not find.
  • 驰马远防 - Ride far away to search.
  • 野中 - In the wilderness.
  • 无所识 - Unrecognized.
  • 兔鸣 - Rabbit cry or sound.
  • 盈掬 - Full in the hand.
  • 经月余 - After several months.
  • 不食而死 - Starve to death.
  • 悲痛甚 - Grieve deeply.
  • 以为畋猎杀害之报也 - Believe it is retribution for hunting and killing.
  • 合家持斋 - The whole family fasts.
  • 不复食肉 - No longer eat meat.
  • 大理寺丞 - A high-ranking official in the imperial court.
  • 蔡宜明 - The name of the official.
  • 代府法曹 - A legal official in the Dai region.
  • 亲说之 - Personally told.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • The *Tai Ping Guang Ji* is a comprehensive collection of stories and anecdotes compiled by Li Fang and others during the Song Dynasty, reflecting various aspects of Chinese culture and history.
  • A high-ranking military position in ancient China, indicating a significant role in the armed forces.
  • An ancient Chinese region, now part of Shanxi Province, known for its strategic importance.
  • The end of the Kaihuang era (581-618 AD) of the Sui Dynasty, a period of significant political and cultural development.
  • An ancient city in Shanxi Province, known for its historical significance.
  • Hunting, often associated with the nobility and military elite.
  • A common theme in Chinese literature and folklore, often used to explore themes of gender imbalance and social expectations.
  • A term used to describe a person who is both beautiful and virtuous.
  • A common phrase used to describe a person who is universally loved and pitied.
  • A phrase indicating deep parental affection.
  • Returning to one's hometown, often associated with nostalgia and family ties.
  • A specific age, often used to highlight the innocence and vulnerability of a child.
  • Disappearance, often used in stories to create suspense and mystery.
  • A common scenario in Chinese literature where neighbors playfully hide a child, often leading to humorous or dramatic situations.
  • A phrase indicating a failed search, often used to build tension.
  • A phrase describing a determined search, often used to show the urgency and effort involved.
  • A specific distance, often used to emphasize the extent of the search.
  • The wilderness, often associated with danger and isolation.
  • Unrecognized, often used to describe a state of confusion or disorientation.
  • A specific sound, often used to create a vivid and eerie atmosphere.
  • Thorny bushes, often used to describe a harsh and unforgiving environment.
  • A specific quantity, often used to describe a large amount.
  • A specific duration, often used to emphasize the passage of time.
  • Starving to death, often used to describe a tragic and inevitable fate.
  • Deep grief, often used to convey intense emotional pain.
  • A belief in retribution, often used to explore themes of karma and justice.
  • Fasting as a form of penance, often used to show the family's commitment to moral and spiritual growth.
  • Giving up meat, often used to symbolize a change in lifestyle or beliefs.
  • A high-ranking official in the imperial court, responsible for legal matters.
  • The name of a historical figure, known for his role as a legal official.
  • A legal official in the Dai region, indicating a specific administrative role.
  • A phrase indicating personal testimony, often used to lend credibility to a story.

Proper Nouns

  • 太平广记 - The title of the collection.
  • 卷一百三十一 - The specific volume and chapter number.
  • 骁骑将军 - The military rank of the protagonist.
  • 王某 - The name of the protagonist, with '王' being the family name.
  • 代郡 - The region where the protagonist is from.
  • 隋开皇末年 - The time period of the story.
  • 蒲州 - The city where the protagonist served.
  • 蔡宜明 - The name of the historical figure who provided the account.
  • 代府法曹 - The position held by P.

[1626]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 少好畋猎 - From a young age enjoyed hunting.
  • 善放鹰犬 - Skilled at using trained hawks and dogs for hunting.
  • 见羣鸟千数 - Saw thousands of birds.
  • 皆无头 - All the birds were headless.
  • 急还我头来 - A plea for the return of their heads.
  • 头痛气絶 - Severe headache and loss of breath.
  • 终身絶酒肉 - A lifelong commitment to abstaining from alcohol and meat.
  • 不杀生命 - A vow not to take any lives.
  • 为邻人牛犯其稼 - Because a neighbor's cow trespassed on someone's crops.
  • 以绳勒牛舌断 - Cutting the cow's tongue with a rope.
  • 并皆瘖哑 - All three sons were mute.
  • 不能言 - Unable to speak.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 隋鹰扬郎将 - A military rank in the Sui Dynasty.
  • 天水姜畧 - A person named Jiang Lüe from Tianshui.
  • 武德中 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozu of Tang.
  • 隰州大宁人 - A place name in the Tang Dynasty.
  • 遂安公李寿 - A person named Li Shou with the title 'Suian Gong'.
  • 贞观初 - The reign title of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
  • 交州都督 - A military governor in the Tang Dynasty.
  • 偏风 - A term for paralysis, often associated with stroke.
  • 追福 - To pray for blessings or perform good deeds for the deceased.

Proper Nouns

  • 隋鹰扬郎将 - A military rank in the Sui Dynasty.
  • 天水姜畧 - A person named Jiang Lüe from Tianshui.
  • 武德中 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozu of Tang.
  • 隰州大宁人 - A place name in the Tang Dynasty.
  • 遂安公李寿 - A person named Li Shou with the title 'Suian Gong'.
  • 贞观初 - The reign title of Emperor Taizong of Tang.
  • 交州都督 - A military governor in the Tang Dynasty.
  • 偏风 - A term for paralysis.
  • 追福 - A religious practice.

[1627]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 方山开少善弓矢尤好游猎以之为业所杀无数 - Describes Fang Shan Kai's skills and profession, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贞观十一年 - The year 637 AD during the reign of Emperor Taizong of Tang, known for its prosperity and cultural achievements.
  • 佛院 - A Buddhist temple, indicating a place of religious practice.
  • 诵经 - Reciting Buddhist scriptures, a common practice in Buddhism.
  • 念佛 - Reciting the name of Buddha, a form of devotion in Buddhism.
  • 鬼 - Spirits or ghosts, often associated with the supernatural in Chinese culture.
  • 鹰 - Eagles, often seen as symbols of power and freedom in Chinese literature.
  • 蛇 - Snakes, often associated with danger and evil in Chinese folklore.

Proper Nouns

  • 方山开 - A historical figure, likely a real person.
  • 贞观十一年 - The year 637 AD.
  • 佛院 - A Buddhist temple.
  • 诵经 - Reciting Buddhist scriptures.
  • 念佛 - Reciting the name of Buddha.
  • 鬼 - Spirits or ghosts.
  • 鹰 - Eagles.
  • 蛇 - Snakes.

[1628]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 并时疾甚 - Means 'sick at the same time and very seriously,' indicating a simultaneous and severe illness.
  • 宅有鹊巢 - Refers to a magpie's nest in the house, which is often considered auspicious in Chinese culture.
  • 旦夕翔鸣 - Describes the magpie flying and calling every morning and evening, emphasizing its frequent activity.
  • 忿其喧噪 - Means 'to be annoyed by its noisy calls,' showing the brothers' irritation.
  • 断舌而放之 - Means 'to cut off its tongue and release it,' a drastic action taken out of annoyance.
  • 口齿之疾 - Refers to speech difficulties, likely a result of the brothers' actions.
  • 行乞 - Means 'to beg,' indicating extreme poverty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐王遵 - Refers to Wang Zun, a person from the Tang dynasty.
  • 河内 - Refers to He Nei, a region in Henan province.
  • 宣验志 - A historical text that records strange and miraculous events, often used to illustrate moral lessons.
  • 陇西李知礼 - Refers to Li Zhiyi, a person from the Tang dynasty.
  • 陇西 - Refers to Longxi, a region in Gansu province.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 越王入呉國 - Refers to the historical event where the King of Yue, Goujian, retreated to Wu after a defeat.
  • 丹鳥夾王飛 - A metaphorical expression describing a pair of red birds flying beside the king, symbolizing auspicious omens.
  • 起望鳥台 - Refers to the construction of a tower called 'Bird Watching Tower' by Goujian to commemorate the auspicious event.
  • 臨洮長人 - A reference to the appearance of twelve giants in Lin洮, which was seen as an omen.
  • 夷服 - Means 'to wear the clothing of the Yi people,' indicating submission or tribute.
  • 漢高祖曾避項羽于此 - Refers to a historical event where Emperor Gaozu of Han (Liu Bang) hid from Xiang Yu, a rival warlord, in a well.
  • 雙鸠所救 - Describes how two crows saved Liu Bang by perching on the well, signaling his presence.
  • 漢祖避時難 - Refers to Liu Bang hiding during a time of difficulty.
  • 隱身厄此間 - Means 'hiding in this difficult place.'
  • 雙鸠集其上 - Describes the crows gathering on the well.

  • 樊將軍哙問于陸賈 - Refers to a historical dialogue between Fan Kuai, a general, and Lu Jia, a scholar.
  • 受命于天 - A common phrase meaning 'to receive a mandate from heaven,' often used to justify the legitimacy of a ruler.
  • 瑞應 - Refers to auspicious signs or omens.
  • 目瞤 - Means 'eyelid twitching,' considered an omen of good fortune.

  • 燈火花 - Refers to the phenomenon of sparks from a lamp, considered an omen.

  • 午鹊噪 - Refers to crows cawing at noon, considered an omen.

  • 蜘蛛集 - Refers to spiders gathering, considered an omen.
  • 百事喜 - Means 'all things will be happy or successful.'
  • 天命無信 - Refers to the idea that heaven's mandate is not to be trusted blindly.
  • 瑞寶信也 - Means 'auspicious signs are trustworthy.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 水星之精 - Refers to the essence or spirit of Mercury (the planet), a concept that combines astronomical and mythological elements.
  • 化为黑石 - Means 'transformed into a black stone,' a literal description of a supernatural event.
  • 渐有文彩 - Means 'gradually developed patterns or colors,' indicating the stone's changing appearance over time.
  • 忽如雷震 - Means 'suddenly like a thunderclap,' describing a sudden and loud noise.
  • 闻声百余里 - Means 'heard for over a hundred miles,' emphasizing the loudness of the sound.
  • 自立 - Means 'stood up on its own,' suggesting a spontaneous and miraculous event.
  • 白色有牛马仙人及玉环玉玦文字之像 - Describes the stone's appearance with white patterns resembling cattle, horses, immortals, jade rings, jade pendants, and written characters.
  • 白燕 - Refers to a white swallow, a symbol of good fortune.
  • 以为神物 - Means 'considered a divine object,' indicating the belief in the swallow's supernatural significance.
  • 以金笼盛置于宫中 - Means 'placed in a golden cage in the palace,' describing the treatment of the white swallow.
  • 旬日不知所在 - Means 'after ten days, it disappeared,' indicating the sudden disappearance of the swallow.
  • 论者以晋金德之瑞 - Means 'scholars considered it a sign of the golden virtue of the Jin dynasty,' a reference to the belief in the swallow's symbolic importance.
  • 师旷时有白燕来巢 - Means 'during the time of Shi Kuang, a white swallow came to build a nest,' referring to a historical event.
  • 以为瑞应 - Means 'considered a good omen,' indicating the positive interpretation of the event.
  • 晋武帝为抚军时 - Means 'when Emperor Wu of Jin was serving as the General of the Left,' providing historical context.
  • 府内后堂忽生草三株 - Means 'three plants suddenly grew in the inner hall of the government office,' describing a supernatural event.
  • 茎黄叶绿 - Means 'with yellow stems and green leaves,' a literal description of the plants.
  • 总金抽翠花条冉弱似金防 - Describes the plants' appearance with golden stems, green leaves, and delicate flowers that resemble gold threads.
  • 有羌人桃馥字世芬 - Introduces a person named Tao Fu, also known as Shi Fen, who was a Qiang ethnic minority person.
  • 在廏中养马 - Means 'in the stables, raising horses,' providing background information about Tao Fu.
  • 云此草应金德之瑞 - Means 'he said this plant corresponds to the golden virtue of the Jin dynasty,' a reference to the belief in the plant's symbolic importance.
  • 帝以草赐张华 - Means 'the emperor gave the plant to Zhang Hua,' describing the emperor's action.
  • 华作金防赋 - Means 'Zhang Hua wrote an ode to the golden threads,' describing Zhang Hua's literary work.
  • 翫九茎于汉庭 - Means 'playfully observed nine stems in the Han court,' a reference to a historical event.
  • 美三株于兹馆 - Means 'admired three plants in this pavilion,' describing the appreciation of the plants.
  • 贵表祥乎金德 - Means 'valuable as a sign of the golden virtue,' indicating the symbolic importance of the plants.
  • 比名而相乱 - Means 'compared to other names and mixed up,' indicating the complexity of the plants' symbolism.

  • 占者云 - The diviner said.
  • 鸡者酉酉者金夫雀变而来赴之即王践祚之象也 - The chicken represents the west, and the west represents gold. The sparrows changing and coming to it is a sign of the king ascending the throne.
  • 元帝时三雀共登一雄鸡背 - During Emperor Yuan's reign, three sparrows climbed onto the back of a rooster.
  • 占者以为当进三爵为天子 - The diviner believed that it would lead to the emperor being promoted three times.

  • 见宕渠 - He saw Dangqu.
  • 秦史胡毋敬曰 - The Qin historian Hu Mu Jing said.
  • 是后五百年外必有异人为大人者 - Five hundred years after this, there will definitely be a strange person who becomes a great man.
  • 及李雄之王 - When Li Xiong became king.
  • 其祖出自宕渠 - His ancestor came from Dangqu.
  • 有识者皆以为应焉 - All knowledgeable people believed it to be a fulfillment.
  • 晋安帝时冀州桑门释法珍告其弟子普严 - During the reign of Emperor An of Jin, the monk Fa Zhen of Ji State told his disciple Pu Yan.
  • 嵩山神告我江东有刘将军汉家苗裔当受天命 - The god of Mount Song told me that there is a General Liu in Jiangdong who is a descendant of the Han dynasty and will receive the mandate of heaven.
  • 吾以璧三十二枚并镇金一饼与之 - I gave him thirty-two jade disks and a piece of gold.
  • 刘氏卜代之数也 - This is the number of generations for the Liu family to rule.
  • 严告同学法义 - Fa Zhen told his fellow students Fa Yi.
  • 以安帝义熙十三年于嵩庙石坛下得宝璧三十二枚 - In the thirteenth year of Emperor An's Yixi era, they found thirty-two jade disks at the stone altar of the Mount Song Temple.
  • 三十二者世宋有天下相承八帝享祚六十年 - Thirty-two represents the Song dynasty having the world for eight emperors for sixty years.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sentence '后唐明宗皇帝微时随蕃将李存信巡边宿于雁门逆旅' describes the emperor's early life and his stay at an inn in Yanmen Pass.
  • The phrase '逆旅媪方娠' ('an old woman at the inn who was pregnant') places '方娠' (was pregnant) after '逆旅媪' (an old woman at the inn).
  • The phrase '腹中儿语谓母曰' ('the child in her womb spoke to his mother, saying') is a complex construction that requires careful translation to maintain the original meaning.
  • The phrase '清泰之在岐阳也' ('when Qingtai was in Qiyang') sets the context for the story.
  • The phrase '云有使者拘录引出防间' ('said that there were messengers who took him out of the underworld') is a metaphorical expression.
  • The phrase '与吾言于潞王' ('tell the Duke of Lü what I say') is a direct instruction from Lord Yin.
  • The phrase '速返达吾之防言' ('quickly convey my instructions to the Duke of Lü') emphasizes the urgency of the message.
  • The phrase '及苏遂以其事密白王之左右' ('when he revived, he secretly told the Duke of Lü's attendants') describes the actions taken by the deceased man after his revival.
  • The phrase '咸以妖妄而莫之信' ('all considered it a superstition and did not believe it') reflects the skepticism of the attendants.
  • The phrase '由是不得闻于王' ('therefore, the Duke of Lü did not hear about it') explains the consequence of the attendants' disbelief.
  • The phrase '月余又暴卒' ('a month later, he died again') indicates a recurring event.
  • The phrase '入防复见阴君' ('he went to the underworld and saw Lord Yin again') continues the narrative in the underworld.
  • The phrase '阴君怒而责之' ('Lord Yin became angry and rebuked him') shows the reaction of Lord Yin.
  • The phrase '何故受吾教而竟不能达耶' ('why did you not convey my instructions?') is a direct question from Lord Yin.
  • The phrase '徐曰放汝去可速导吾言' ('he said slowly, 'I will let you go, quickly convey my words.') is a response from Lord Yin.
  • The phrase '仍请王画吾形及地藏菩萨像' ('also ask the Duke of Lü to draw my image and the image of Ksitigarbha Bodhisattva') is an additional request from Lord Yin.
  • The phrase '何惶恐' ('He was very frightened') describes the emotional state of the deceased man.

[1698]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 郊坛下有一白狗高三尺光色鲜明 - Describes a white dog standing three feet tall with bright and vivid colors, located at the foot of the altar.
  • 恒卧坛侧觉见人前则去 - The dog usually lies down beside the altar and leaves when it senses people approaching.
  • 骑督王琬以骏马追之狗徐行马不可及 - Wang Wan, a horse supervisor, tried to catch the dog with a fast horse, but the dog moved slowly, making it impossible for the horse to catch up.
  • 射又逃琬去复还 - Wang Wan tried to shoot the dog, but it escaped again. After he left, the dog returned.
  • 郊丘非狗所守后遂大乱 - The dog was not supposed to be guarding the altar, and later, chaos ensued.
  • 幽州有狗鼻行地三百余步 - Describes a dog from Youzhou that could walk on its nose for more than 300 steps.
  • 归何早防牛人立而行 - Why did you return so early? The ox stands upright and walks.
  • 天下将乱非止一家 - Predicts that the world will be in chaos and it won't be limited to one family.
  • 五州残乱聘方族灭 - Five states were in turmoil, and Zhang Peng's clan was eventually destroyed.
  • 天下饥荒狗行欲倒 - A time of famine where dogs were so hungry they were about to collapse.
  • 天下人饥死狗语不已闻者怖走 - The dog kept speaking about the starving people, and those who heard it were terrified and fled.
  • 帝没于胡 - The emperor was captured by the Xiongnu (the 'Hu' people).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 晋武帝太康七年 - The seventh year of the Taikang era of Emperor Wu of Jin.
  • 王琬 - A historical figure, Wang Wan, who served as a horse supervisor during the Jin dynasty.
  • 张聘 - Zhang Peng, a historical figure from Jiangxia.
  • 张昌 - Zhang Chang, a rebel leader during the Jin dynasty.
  • 江夏 - A region in present-day Hubei province.
  • 幽州 - An ancient Chinese state, roughly corresponding to modern-day Beijing and northern Hebei.
  • 胡 - A term used to refer to the Xiongnu, a nomadic people who often clashed with the Han Chinese.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 狗行欲倒 - This phrase can be ambiguous. It could mean that the dog was about to collapse due to hunger, or that the dog was walking in a way that seemed to be about to fall.
  • 天下将乱非止一家 - This could be interpreted as a prediction of widespread chaos that would affect more than one family or as a statement about the nature of chaos.
  • 张昌作乱先略江夏众推为帅 - The sentence structure is somewhat elliptical, but it clearly describes Zhang Chang's rebellion and the subsequent selection of him as a leader.
  • 五州残乱聘方族灭 - This sentence is also elliptical, but it indicates that five states were in turmoil and Zhang Peng's clan was eventually destroyed.

Proper Nouns

  • 晋武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin.
  • 王琬 - Wang Wan.
  • 张聘 - Zhang Peng.
  • 张昌 - Zhang Chang.
  • 江夏 - Jiangxia.
  • 幽州 - Youzhou.
  • 胡 - Xiongnu.

[1700]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 帝入宮方没 - When the emperor entered the palace, the fish submerged; suggests a symbolic connection between the fish's behavior and the emperor's actions.
  • 此下人將舉兵睥睨乘舆之象 - This was a sign that people below would rebel and challenge the imperial carriage; '睥睨' means to look down upon or challenge.
  • 防有侯景之亂 - And indeed, there was the rebellion of Hou Jing; '防' here means 'indeed' or 'as it turned out.'
  • 骑一竹枝為馬 - Rode a bamboo stick as if it were a horse; describes a dynamic and unusual movement.
  • 振防驰驿盤躃回轉 - Shook his staff and galloped, spinning and turning; describes a dynamic and unusual movement.
  • 或時厲聲雲某處追兵甚急何不差遣 - Sometimes he shouted loudly, saying that there were urgent pursuing troops somewhere and why not dispatch them; sentence structure is elliptical, and the context is implied.
  • 遂放杖驰走不遑寧息 - Then he threw down his staff and ran, without stopping to rest; sequence of actions is dynamic and rapid.
  • 如其所言果有烽檄之急 - As he said, there were indeed urgent military alerts; connection between the monk's words and the actual military alerts is direct.
  • 毎遙見黑雲飛烏羣豕 - Whenever he saw dark clouds, flying crows, and groups of pigs; vivid and somewhat surreal description.
  • 但是黑之物必低身恭敬 - Whenever he saw anything black, he would lower his body in respect; behavior of the monk when seeing black objects is unusual.
  • 忽自稱雲伏喽啰 - Suddenly he would call himself 'Fu Luluo'; sudden change in the monk's self-identification is surprising.
  • 語國人見者莫不怪笑 - When the people of the country saw him, they would all find it strange and laugh; reaction of the people is described in a way that highlights their surprise and amusement.
  • 京內咸識不知名字者呼為竹馬顚僧 - In the capital, everyone recognized him, but those who didn't know his name called him the 'Bamboo Horse Mad Monk'; nickname given to the monk is a cultural reference.
  • 齊未動之前惠炤走杖馬來到殿西騎省密告諸貴唐邕等急救東方呉兒大欲入 - Before the Qi army moved, Master Huizhao ran with his staff and horse to the west palace and secretly informed the nobles, including Tang Yong, to urgently save the eastern Wu people who were about to enter; sequence of events and the urgency of the situation are emphasized.
  • 曉夕孜孜守闕不去數日 - Day and night, he diligently guarded the palace gate for several days; monk's dedication and persistence are highlighted.
  • 國家遣兵將救始集兵馬 - The state sent troops to rescue, and the soldiers began to gather; response of the state is described in a concise manner.
  • 惠炤已去城四十里於白壁南待軍指麾號令 - Master Huizhao had already gone forty miles south of the city wall and waited for the army to give orders; monk's position and his role in waiting for the army are clear.
  • 在京百官朝集惠炤亦騎杖執防立於武成之 - In the capital, all the officials gathered for court, and Master Huizhao also rode his staff and held his staff, standing at the Wu Cheng (Military Achievement) Hall; monk's presence at the court and his actions are described in a formal setting.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 周靖帝大象中 - Refers to the period during the reign of Emperor Jing of Zhou in the 'Daxiang' era.
  • 聞園中有犬聲 - Literally means 'heard a dog barking in the garden,' used to describe an unusual event.
  • 鬭久之 - Means 'fought for a long time,' indicating a prolonged conflict.
  • 黑者周所尚色也 - Means 'the black one was the color favored by the Zhou,' indicating a symbolic meaning.
  • 死者灭亡之象 - Means 'the dead one is a symbol of destruction,' suggesting a metaphorical interpretation.
  • 隋有天下旗牲尚赤戎服尚黄 - Means 'the Sui dynasty had the flag and sacrificial animals favoring red and the military attire favoring yellow,' indicating the symbolic colors of the Sui dynasty.
  • 突厥阿波未叛之前 - Refers to the period before the rebellion of the Turkic leader Apa.
  • 山上有鹿临崖告人云 - Means 'a deer on the mountain by the cliff told people,' indicating a supernatural or metaphorical event.
  • 你等无事触他南方圣人之国 - Means 'you are causing trouble for the southern sage's country,' suggesting a warning.
  • 俄而国内大乱 - Means 'soon the country fell into chaos,' indicating a rapid change in circumstances.
  • 陈后主时秣陵有泉深不可测 - Refers to the period of Emperor Chen Houzhu, describing a deep, mysterious spring in Moling.
  • 产鱼鳖甚众 - Means 'produced many fish and turtles,' indicating the spring's fertility.
  • 恒有声如牛 - Means 'always had a sound like a cow,' describing an unusual noise.
  • 邑人惧之不敢犯 - Means 'the people of the town were afraid and dared not touch it,' indicating fear.
  • 无何忽见牛头于岸下 - Means 'soon they saw a cow's head on the shore,' describing a sudden and unusual event.
  • 里民牵而出之 - Means 'the villagers pulled it out,' indicating an action taken by the villagers.
  • 于是争捕其鱼乃尽 - Means 'then they competed to catch the fish, and they all disappeared,' indicating a result of the villagers' actions.
  • 江东旧以牛头山为天关 - Means 'in the east of the river, the Niu Tou Mountain was called the Heavenly Pass,' indicating a symbolic name.
  • 今牛头已获盖示国将灭而关毁也 - Means 'now that the cow's head has been captured, it shows that the country will be destroyed and the pass will be ruined,' suggesting a metaphorical interpretation.
  • 后年隋平陈 - Means 'the next year, the Sui dynasty conquered Chen,' indicating a historical event.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 虾防跃出色如黄金背有朱书武字 - This sentence describes a golden shrimp-like creature with red characters on its back, which is a metaphorical or symbolic description.
  • 宫人不敢匿奏之 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'the palace lady dared not hide it and reported it.'
  • 帝颇惊异遽命放于苑池 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'the emperor was greatly surprised and immediately ordered it to be released into the imperial garden.'
  • 武退在口中与囚字无异 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'the character '武' in the mouth is similar to the character '囚,' which means 'prison.'
  • 则天大喜下制即依月余有上封者云 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'Empress Wu was very pleased and issued an edict to follow the suggestion. After a month, someone else submitted a petition saying...'
  • 武后竟革命 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'Empress Wu eventually led a revolution.'
  • 天授中则天好改新字 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'During the Tian Shou period, Empress Wu liked to create new characters.'
  • 有幽州人防如意上封云 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'A person from Youzhou named Fang Ruishui submitted a petition saying...'
  • 国字中或或乱天象请口中安武以鎭之 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'The character '国' (guo) is chaotic and disrupts the celestial order. Please place '武' (wu) in the mouth to stabilize it.'
  • 则天大喜下制即依月余有上封者云 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'Empress Wu was very pleased and issued an edict to follow the suggestion. After a month, someone else submitted a petition saying...'
  • 武退在口中与囚字无异不祥之甚则天愕然遽追制改令中为八方字 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'The character '武' in the mouth is similar to the character '囚,' which means 'prison.' This is very inauspicious. Empress Wu was shocked and immediately issued an edict to change the middle character to '八方' (bafang).'
  • 孝和即位果幽则天于上阳宫 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'Emperor Zhongzong ascended the throne and indeed confined Empress Wu to the Shangyang Palace.'
  • 黙啜贼到并州至十五日夜月蚀尽贼并退尽 - The sentence is elliptical, meaning 'The Khitan invaders reached Bingzhou and on the night of the fifteenth day, a total lunar eclipse occurred. The invaders then retreated completely.'
  • 俗谚云枣子塞鼻孔悬楼阁却种又云蝉鸣蛁蟟唤黍种餻糜断又谚云春雨甲子赤地千里夏雨甲子乘船入市秋雨甲子禾头生耳鹊巢下近地其年大水 - The sentence is a series of folk sayings and predictions, each with its own structure and meaning.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '使人于中庭洗车卸辕晒之' is elliptical and could be interpreted as a ritual or a mundane act with deeper meaning.
  • The phrase '有飘风中门而入' is ambiguous in terms of whether the whirlwind is literal or symbolic.
  • The sentence '夜有人扣阁而呼曰府君今可去矣' is elliptical, and the identity of the caller is unclear.
  • The timing and cause of the rebellion are not explicitly stated in '俄而刺史南郡王义宣作逆'.
  • The sequence of events is compressed, and the relationship between the snakes and Wang Yan's fate is unclear in '其将及祸也见屋桷悉是大蛇就视之则灭焉'.
  • The sequence of events is complex and involves multiple supernatural occurrences leading to Liu Chong's downfall in '后宠为折冲将军受命北征将行而炊饭尽变为虫其家人蒸麨亦变为虫其火逾猛其虫逾壮宠遂北征军败于坛丘为徐龙所杀'.

Wenzhao rallied his troops and fought fiercely. His subordinates fought to the death, and two of them died by sunset. They killed eight hundred pursuers, but Wenzhao was defeated. Idiomatic Expressions

  • 尝为汜水县令 - Classical expression meaning 'once served as the magistrate of Fan Shui County.'

  • 果入为著作郎 - 'Indeed, he later became a *Zuo Zuo Lang*.' *著作郎* is a classical title, often translated as 'Officer of the Imperial Academy.'
  • 配流而身亡也 - 'was exiled and died.' *配流* refers to exiling someone to a remote area.
  • 怀姙过期数月不产 - 'Pregnant for several months past her due date without giving birth.' *怀姙* is an archaic term for 'pregnant.'
  • 愼勿服药 - 'Be careful not to take medicine.' *愼* is an archaic form of *慎* (cautious).
  • 合改畿令十五月作御史中丞 - 'After fifteen months as a *Ji Ling* (capital magistrate), he will become *Yu Shi Zhongcheng* (Imperial Censor-in-Chief).' *合改* may mean 'will be changed to' or 'will be promoted to.'

  • 又闻空中曰勿惊司空霆火登止俄复藂集 - Command from the sky, followed by a description of thunder and fire that stops and then gathers again. '藂集' means 'gathers in a cluster.'
  • 又闻空中曰勿惊太尉既而阴翳廓然终无所损 - Command from the sky, followed by a description of a sudden darkness that clears, causing no harm. '阴翳' means 'darkness,' '廓然' means 'clears completely,' and '终无所损' means 'causes no harm in the end.'
  • 然死虵从马则已失矣去逸自坐须富贵不数年染疾而卒官至太仆卿 - Describes a snake that dies, and a horse that is lost. '去逸自坐须富贵不数年染疾而卒官至太仆卿' is difficult to parse, but it seems to describe someone who becomes wealthy and holds a high office (太仆卿) but eventually dies from illness. '自坐' is unclear and may be a typo or corruption.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 顧見向者持塔僧 - 顧見 means 'to look back and see,' 向者 refers to 'the one who came before,' and 持塔僧 means 'the monk carrying the pagoda.' This phrase describes a scene where the narrator looks back and sees a monk who had previously carried a pagoda.
  • 無由歸矣 - Literally means 'no way to return,' indicating that the monk cannot return home or fulfill his original mission.
  • 少殷曰 - 少殷 is a personal name, likely a character in the story.
  • 饑甚柰何 - 饑甚 means 'very hungry,' and 捺何 is an interrogative particle meaning 'what to do?' or 'how to handle it?'
  • 唯蜜煎姜可食 - 唯 means 'only,' 蜜煎姜 refers to candied ginger, and 可食 means 'can be eaten.' This phrase indicates that the only thing the monk can eat is candied ginger.
  • 乃取食之 - 乃 means 'then,' 取 means 'to take,' and 食之 means 'to eat it.' This phrase describes the action of taking and eating the candied ginger.
  • 王判官竟不至 - 王判官 is a title, likely a government official, and 竟不至 means 'did not come at all.' This phrase indicates that the official did not arrive as expected.
  • 可去矣 - 可去 means 'can leave,' and 矣 is a particle indicating a completed action. This phrase suggests that it is time to leave.
  • 復出詰兄 - 復出 means 'went out again,' 詰 means 'to question,' and 兄 means 'brother.' This phrase describes the narrator going out again to question his brother.
  • 泣且請去 - 泣 means 'to cry,' 且 means 'and,' and 請去 means 'to ask to leave.' This phrase indicates that the narrator cried and asked to leave.
  • 兄知不可留 - 兄 means 'brother,' 知 means 'knows,' 不可 means 'cannot,' and 留 means 'to stay.' This phrase indicates that the brother knew he could not keep the narrator.
  • 乃入白官府 - 乃 means 'then,' 入 means 'to enter,' and 白官府 means 'to report to the authorities.' This phrase describes the brother entering and reporting to the authorities.
  • 許之 - 許 means 'to permit,' and 之 is a particle referring to the narrator. This phrase indicates that the authorities permitted the narrator to leave.
  • 既得歸人間 - 既 means 'already,' 得 means 'to obtain,' 歸 means 'to return,' and 人間 means 'the human world.' This phrase indicates that the narrator has already returned to the human world.
  • 願知當為何官 - 願 means 'to wish,' 知 means 'to know,' 當為 means 'to be,' and 何官 means 'what official position.' This phrase indicates that the narrator wishes to know what official position he will hold.
  • 兄曰 - 兄 means 'brother,' and 曰 means 'said.' This phrase introduces the brother's response.
  • 此甚難言 - 此 means 'this,' 甚 means 'very,' 難 means 'difficult,' and 言 means 'to say.' This phrase indicates that the brother finds it very difficult to say.
